column1: # boolean.tsv — extensive tests for Boolean coercion, short-circuiting,
# algebraic type Operators (tor / tand), the `otherwise` word, and
# their interactions with the rest of the language.
# Lines starting with # and empty lines are ignored.
# Numbers: non-zero is true, zero is false.
# Strings: "true"/"false" parse literally; otherwise non-empty is true.
# Numbers: returns the actual operand value.
# Numbers: first falsy wins; otherwise last truthy.
# Double negation via parens (chained `not not` is not how AQL parses).
# 5. xor / nand / nor / iff — coerce, return boolean
# false is falsy but not None.
# None: behaves the same way in or vs otherwise (both return the right).
Integer tor Decimal tor String tor None
# Concrete values produce a disjunct of values.
# Disjunct alternatives are kept as-is at runtime (no subsumption).
# Disjunct unifies with one of its alternatives.
# Type literals: tand falls back to Unify; the narrower type Wins.
# Merge of disjuncts: each merge picks an alternative — tested via unify.
# tor inside an optional record field.
# Use parens to force evaluation order — `or`/`and` have BarrierPos
# scoring that interacts with chaining; explicit grouping is clearer.
# 2-arg if with falsy condition returns nothing.
# `otherwise` for default values inside if branches.
# Arithmetic on the winning value of `or`/`and`.
# `otherwise` only swaps in the fallback when the first arg is `None`;
# the empty string passes through (distinct from `or`).
# 17. otherwise with optional record fields (None defaults)
# A field that resolves to None gets a fallback via `otherwise`.
type Item record [name:String desc:[String tor None]] (make Item ["a" None]) get desc
((make (record [name:String desc:[String tor None]]) ["a" None]) get desc) otherwise "no description"
((make (record [name:String desc:[String tor None]]) ["a" "set"]) get desc) otherwise "no description"
# not on a coerced disjunct (the disjunct itself is truthy).
# tor with a single arg via chaining.
# 19. any — list-or, short-circuit, returns winning element
# Empty list returns identity (false for OR).
# Coerced via convert-boolean rules: empty list inside list is falsy.
# 20. all — list-and, short-circuit, returns winning element
# Numeric lists — first falsy wins, else last.
# Empty list returns identity (true for AND).
# Empty inner list is falsy for `all`.
# Use any/all as a condition (wrap the quantifier in parens so its
# result feeds into `if`, not the raw list).
# Combine with otherwise — `any` returns 0 (last falsy), then otherwise
# replaces only on None, not on falsy.
# any over a list built from comparisons.
# all over a list built from comparisons.
# Single-element list returns the element unchanged.
String tor Number tor Boolean tor None
# Use a tany result with unify.
# Single-element list returns the element unchanged.
# Non-map values fall back to Unify.
# Type literal narrowing across the list.
# tany a list with computed disjuncts.
# Use tall to fold a list of map schemas, then unify.
# tany an empty disjunct via single-element collapses.
# Negative numbers are truthy (only literal zero is falsy).
# 26. any/all — scalar edge cases (strings, atoms, none)
# String literal "true"/"false" coerce to actual booleans.
# Strings that look like numbers don't coerce numerically — non-empty
# Empty list inside list is falsy.
# 29. any/all — interactions with not, otherwise, if
# `not [list] any` → "all elements are falsy" — but `not` operates on
# the `any` result, which is a value (not a boolean). It coerces.
# any with otherwise — `any` returns the falsy operand (e.g. 0); since
# 0 is not None, otherwise keeps it.
# Use as `if` condition (parens force the quantifier to evaluate
# A def-bound list auto-evaluates when referenced, splicing onto the
# stack — so to use any/all on a stored list, build the list inline
# any/all over a parens-grouped expression that produces a list.
# any short-circuits — a "side-effecting" element after the truthy
# winner is irrelevant since values are already evaluated. Confirm
# the result is the first truthy element regardless of order.
# all short-circuits to the first falsy.
# Repeated falsy values collapse to the last-seen.
# Single-element list returns the element unchanged for tany/tall.
# Single-element with an existing disjunct returns it unchanged.
# tany of values around a disjunct preserves order.

# tany unify with a value matching the disjunct.
# Type narrows down through the chain.
# Three-way merge with one shared key.
# Order: earlier keys appear first in result order.
# 37. tall — disjoint cases reduce to Never
# Two different concrete values for the same key are disjoint, so the
# whole intersection collapses to Never (the bottom type / empty
# intersection). tall is total: it never errors on disjoint input.
# Non-map values that don't unify also reduce to Never.
# Subtype wins: narrower type Is kept.
# tany result fed into is/unify chains.
# tall produces a plain merged map (not a record type), so unify is
# the right way to check it satisfies a target shape.
# tany schema as an `Options` field type (note: Options prints lowercase).
# Build a disjunct of merged maps.
# Use tany inside tall (combined into a single field).
# tall then tany then tall — composable.
# Define a named disjunct via tany.
# Use the named disjunct as a record field constraint.
type Maybe tany [String None] type Person record [name:String nick:Maybe] make Person {name:"Alice" nick:None}
type Maybe tany [String None] type Person record [name:String nick:Maybe] make Person {name:"Bob" nick:"Bobby"}
# Boolean reduction over a list of computed `is` checks.
[(1 is Integer) (true is Boolean) ("a" is String)] all
[(1 is Integer) (true is Boolean) ("a" is None)] any
